answer:
**The work is *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I* by Charles Dunoyer, chapter 11 ("I. Liberté compatible avec la manière de vivre des peuples purement industrieux.").**[^1]

This chapter reconstructs the etymology of "industry" from Latin *INDUSTRIA*, derived from *INDU* (for *INTUS*, "within") + *STRUO* ("I arrange, I set up, I construct"), and explains its initial application to cunning or fraud (e.g., "knights of industry" for swindlers).[^2] It cites the Abbé Galiani's 1750 claim that fortunes grow only at others' expense.[^3]

The requested passage on the *INTUS*-*STRUO* derivation is:

> The word *industry* is a nearly literal translation of the Latin word INDUSTRIA. Etymologists suppose that the latter must have been formed from two others, INDU for INTUS, *within*, and STRUO, *I arrange, I set up, I construct*. Thus one would have said INDUSTRIA from INTUS-STRUO, INTUS-STRUERE, *to construct within*, an action exercised in things or on things to arrive at a certain end.[^4]

[^1]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [ch.11](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/11-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/).

[^2]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [ch.11, p-b3a2fd](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/11-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/#p-b3a2fd).

[^3]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [ch.11, p-314852](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/11-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/#p-314852).

[^4]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [ch.11, p-06038f](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/11-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/#p-06038f).
